title: Business Negotiation
description: Practice professional business negotiation conversations in English

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: Thank you for coming in today. We're very interested in your software platform, but before we move forward, we need to discuss the terms more carefully.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: Of course. We appreciate your interest and we're committed to making this work for both parties. What are your main concerns?

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: First, the pricing. Your proposal came in at $120,000 annually, which is above our budgeted range of $90,000 to $100,000.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: I understand. The $120,000 includes premium support, unlimited users, and custom integrations. If we narrow the scope slightly, we can work within your range. For example, if you're comfortable with standard support and a limit of 200 users, we could offer the package at $95,000.

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: That's closer. We currently have 150 users but anticipate growth. Could we negotiate a tiered pricing model so costs scale gradually as we add users?

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: Absolutely. We can structure it so the first 200 users are $95,000, then $40 per additional user per year beyond that. That protects your budget now while accommodating growth.

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: That's reasonable. Now, regarding the implementation timeline — you proposed twelve weeks. Our operations team needs the system live by the end of Q2, which is nine weeks away.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: Nine weeks is aggressive but achievable if we prioritize core modules and defer the reporting dashboard to a phase two rollout at no additional cost. Would that be acceptable?

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: We could accept a phased rollout if the core modules include user management, billing, and client portal. Those are non-negotiable for Q2.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: Confirmed. Those three modules will be live within nine weeks. The reporting and analytics dashboard would follow in an additional six weeks.

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: Good. Let's talk about support. The standard support package is business hours only, but we operate across multiple time zones and need coverage around the clock.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: 24/7 support is part of our premium tier, which adds $18,000 annually. However, given the contract size and the multi-year commitment you mentioned, I can offer 24/7 support at $10,000 extra, bringing the total to $105,000.

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: We're willing to consider a two-year commitment, but we'd need a 5% price reduction in year two as an incentive.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: I can offer 3% in year two. That reflects the cost savings from a committed contract but maintains our service quality.

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: We'll accept 3% in year two. There's one more item — data ownership. Our legal team needs assurance that all data remains our property and can be fully exported upon contract termination.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: Absolutely. That's standard in our contracts. You own all your data. Upon termination, we provide a full data export in CSV and JSON formats within 30 days at no charge.

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: That works. I think we're close to an agreement. Let me summarize: $105,000 year one with 24/7 support, 200-user base with tiered pricing, phased rollout in nine weeks for core modules, and 3% discount in year two.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: That's an accurate summary. I'll draft the revised contract and send it to you by end of day Thursday. Shall we schedule a call next Monday to review?

lines.speaker: Client
lines.text: Monday at 10 AM works perfectly. We're looking forward to partnering with you.

lines.speaker: Vendor
lines.text: Likewise. We're excited to bring value to your team.

lines.speaker: HR Manager
lines.text: Congratulations — we'd like to extend you an offer for the Senior Product Manager role. We're very excited about having you join the team.

lines.speaker: Candidate
lines.text: Thank you so much. I'm genuinely excited about this opportunity. I've given the role a lot of thought and I'm very enthusiastic about joining.

lines.speaker: HR Manager
lines.text: Wonderful. Our offer is a base salary of $110,000, plus a 15% annual performance bonus, health insurance, and four weeks of paid leave.

lines.speaker: Candidate
lines.text: I really appreciate the offer. I was hoping we could discuss the base salary. Based on my research and my seven years of experience managing products at scale, I was expecting closer to $125,000.

lines.speaker: HR Manager
lines.text: I understand. Our current band for this level is $108,000 to $118,000. $125,000 would be above our senior PM band.

lines.speaker: Candidate
lines.text: I appreciate the transparency. I want to be reasonable. Could we meet at $118,000, which is the top of your band? I believe my track record launching three enterprise products with over $2M in ARR each justifies that level.

lines.speaker: HR Manager
lines.text: That's a fair point. I'll need to get approval from our VP, but I'm confident we can reach $118,000. Would that work for you?

lines.speaker: Candidate
lines.text: Yes, that would be acceptable. I also wanted to ask about the equity component — is there stock or options included?

lines.speaker: HR Manager
lines.text: Yes — I should have mentioned it. You'd receive 8,000 stock options vesting over four years with a one-year cliff.

lines.speaker: Candidate
lines.text: That's good to know. Given the growth stage of the company, I was hoping for 10,000 options. Is there flexibility there?

lines.speaker: HR Manager
lines.text: I can propose 9,000 options to our leadership. The additional 1,000 above our standard is something I can advocate for given your background.

lines.speaker: Candidate
lines.text: I appreciate that. What about the start date? I have a 30-day notice period with my current employer.

lines.speaker: HR Manager
lines.text: We can accommodate a 30-day notice. The ideal start would be the first Monday of next month, but we have some flexibility within that week.

lines.speaker: Candidate
lines.text: Perfect. One last thing — is there any flexibility in the work-from-home policy? I currently work remotely three days a week.

lines.speaker: HR Manager
lines.text: Our current policy is two days remote. However, since you'll be traveling to meet customers regularly, it's likely you'd functionally have additional remote days. I can confirm this with your future manager.

lines.speaker: Candidate
lines.text: That's reasonable. Assuming $118,000 base, 9,000 options, and confirmation on the flexible work arrangement, I'm ready to accept.

lines.speaker: HR Manager
lines.text: Excellent! I'll send the updated offer letter by tomorrow morning. Welcome aboard — we're thrilled to have you joining the team.

lines.speaker: Candidate
lines.text: Thank you so much. I look forward to contributing from day one.

lines.speaker: Startup Founder
lines.text: Thank you for meeting with me today, Ms. Nakamura. I'm excited to discuss how a strategic partnership could benefit both our organizations.

lines.speaker: Investor
lines.text: I've read your proposal with interest. Your platform has impressive user growth. Tell me more about what you're looking for in a partner.

lines.speaker: Startup Founder
lines.text: We've grown to 50,000 active users organically, but to reach the next level, we need distribution. Your company has an established network of 200 enterprise clients that would be an ideal audience for our product.

lines.speaker: Investor
lines.text: What's in it for us? Our clients already have existing solutions in place.

lines.speaker: Startup Founder
lines.text: Our analytics show that companies using our tool alongside traditional solutions see a 35% improvement in team productivity. We'd offer your clients an exclusive discount and your company a 15% revenue share on all enterprise subscriptions we generate through your network.

lines.speaker: Investor
lines.text: 15% is a starting point, but given the value of our network and brand credibility, we'd need at least 20%.

lines.speaker: Startup Founder
lines.text: I can do 18% with a performance bonus that brings it to 22% if we exceed a hundred enterprise sign-ups in the first year.

lines.speaker: Investor
lines.text: That's creative. I appreciate the performance incentive. What about exclusivity? Would we be your only distribution partner in this market?

lines.speaker: Startup Founder
lines.text: I can offer six-month exclusivity in the Asia-Pacific market. After that, we'd revisit based on results. Full global exclusivity wouldn't be feasible at this stage.

lines.speaker: Investor
lines.text: Six months is short. Could we extend to twelve months for Asia-Pacific and add first right of refusal for Europe?

lines.speaker: Startup Founder
lines.text: Twelve months for Asia-Pacific is acceptable. For Europe, I can offer a ninety-day first right of refusal window before we approach other partners.

lines.speaker: Investor
lines.text: I think we're getting close. What does the integration look like technically?

lines.speaker: Startup Founder
lines.text: We have a plug-and-play API. Most clients can integrate within two weeks. We'd provide dedicated technical support for your client base at no extra cost during the first year.

lines.speaker: Investor
lines.text: That's reassuring. I'll take this to our board next Thursday. Can you send me the revised proposal with these terms by Monday?

lines.speaker: Startup Founder
lines.text: Absolutely. I'll have the updated proposal in your inbox by Monday morning.

lines.speaker: Buyer
lines.text: Thank you for taking my call. We've been a loyal customer for three years now, and I'd like to discuss our pricing for the upcoming year.

lines.speaker: Supplier
lines.text: Of course, Mr. Garcia. We value your business. What's on your mind?

lines.speaker: Buyer
lines.text: We're planning to increase our order volume by about 40% next year. Naturally, we'd expect a volume discount to reflect that growth.

lines.speaker: Supplier
lines.text: A 40% increase is significant and we'd welcome the additional business. Currently, you're at our standard pricing of $12.50 per unit. For that volume, I could offer $11.75 per unit.

lines.speaker: Buyer
lines.text: I appreciate the gesture, but that's only a 6% reduction for a 40% increase in volume. I was expecting something closer to $10.50 per unit.

lines.speaker: Supplier
lines.text: I understand your position, but our raw material costs have increased by 8% this year. $10.50 would be below our margin threshold.

lines.speaker: Buyer
lines.text: I hear you on the cost pressures. What if we committed to a minimum order quantity per quarter? That would give you predictable demand and reduce your inventory risk.

lines.speaker: Supplier
lines.text: A quarterly commitment would help our planning considerably. If you guarantee minimum orders of 5,000 units per quarter, I could bring the price down to $11.00 per unit.

lines.speaker: Buyer
lines.text: That's more reasonable. One additional request — we've been experiencing some quality inconsistencies in recent shipments. Three of our last ten deliveries had defect rates above 2%.

lines.speaker: Supplier
lines.text: I apologize for that. I'll speak with our quality control team immediately. Would a written quality guarantee with penalty clauses address your concern?

lines.speaker: Buyer
lines.text: Yes. I'd want a maximum defect rate of 1% with replacement at no cost for any batch exceeding that threshold.

lines.speaker: Supplier
lines.text: That's fair. We'll include a quality guarantee in the contract. Can we also discuss payment terms? Currently you're on net-30.

lines.speaker: Buyer
lines.text: We'd prefer to move to net-45 given the larger order sizes.

lines.speaker: Supplier
lines.text: I can offer net-45 if you agree to electronic payments rather than checks. That speeds up our processing.

lines.speaker: Buyer
lines.text: That works for us. So we're looking at $11.00 per unit, minimum 5,000 units per quarter, net-45 with electronic payment, and a 1% defect guarantee. Do we have a deal?

lines.speaker: Supplier
lines.text: We have a deal. I'll send the updated contract by end of week. Thank you for your continued partnership, Mr. Garcia.

lines.speaker: Tenant
lines.text: Good afternoon. I'm interested in the office space on the fourth floor. I understand it's listed at $45 per square foot annually?

lines.speaker: Landlord
lines.text: That's correct. It's 3,000 square feet of modern office space with great natural light and access to shared meeting rooms.

lines.speaker: Tenant
lines.text: The space is ideal for our needs. However, we're a growing startup with twelve employees and we're conscious of costs. Is there room to negotiate on the rate?

lines.speaker: Landlord
lines.text: What lease term are you considering? Longer commitments give us more flexibility on pricing.

lines.speaker: Tenant
lines.text: We're thinking three years. We need stability, but we also want the option to expand if our team grows.

lines.speaker: Landlord
lines.text: For a three-year lease, I can bring the rate down to $42 per square foot. That brings your annual rent to $126,000, or $10,500 per month.

lines.speaker: Tenant
lines.text: We were hoping for closer to $38 per square foot. There are comparable spaces in this neighborhood at that rate.

lines.speaker: Landlord
lines.text: Those spaces don't include the amenities we offer — 24-hour access, on-site parking, and building security. But I'll tell you what — I can offer $40 per square foot if you agree to a three-year term with the first month rent-free as a move-in incentive.

lines.speaker: Tenant
lines.text: The rent-free month is attractive. What about tenant improvements? The space would need some modifications for our open-plan office layout.

lines.speaker: Landlord
lines.text: We provide a tenant improvement allowance of $15 per square foot for leases over two years. That's $45,000 toward your buildout.

lines.speaker: Tenant
lines.text: Our buildout estimate is closer to $60,000. Could you increase the allowance to $20 per square foot?

lines.speaker: Landlord
lines.text: I can go to $18 per square foot, which gives you $54,000. The remaining $6,000 would be your responsibility.

lines.speaker: Tenant
lines.text: That's workable. One last point — we'd like a right of first refusal on the adjacent suite in case we need to expand.

lines.speaker: Landlord
lines.text: I can include that in the lease. If the adjacent space becomes available, you'll have 30 days to decide before we market it to other tenants.

lines.speaker: Tenant
lines.text: Excellent. I'll have my lawyer review the terms and we should be ready to sign within two weeks.

lines.speaker: Landlord
lines.text: Wonderful. I'll have the lease agreement drafted and sent to your attorney by Friday.
